questions.question: What is a static mechanism?
options: A mechanism that runs at runtime
A white-box analysis mechanism that examines source code without executing it
A mechanism that monitors network traffic
questions.explanation: A static mechanism performs white-box analysis — it examines the source code or program structure without executing it. Type checkers are a classic example.

questions.question: What is a dynamic mechanism?
options: A mechanism that analyzes source code
A mechanism that compiles code with extra checks
A black-box mechanism that monitors program behavior at runtime
A mechanism that encrypts data in motion
questions.explanation: A dynamic mechanism is a black-box runtime monitor — it observes the program's behavior during execution. Firewalls and intrusion detection systems are examples.

questions.question: A sound enforcer guarantees:
options: No false positives (never rejects safe programs)
No false negatives (never accepts violating programs)
No false positives AND no false negatives
questions.explanation: A sound enforcer has no false negatives — it never misses a violation. It may have false positives (rejecting safe programs), but it will catch all bad programs.

questions.question: A complete enforcer guarantees:
options: No false positives (never rejects safe programs)
questions.explanation: A complete enforcer has no false positives — it never incorrectly rejects a safe program. It may have false negatives (missing some violations).

questions.question: A precise enforcer would need to be:
options: Both sound and complete — no false positives AND no false negatives
questions.explanation: A precise enforcer is both sound (no false negatives) and complete (no false positives). In general, achieving a precise enforcer is impossible due to the Halting Problem.

questions.question: Why can't a perfect static mechanism exist for all properties?
options: The Halting Problem proves it's impossible to determine all program behaviors statically
Static mechanisms can't read source code
questions.explanation: The Halting Problem proves that no algorithm can determine whether an arbitrary program halts. Since a perfect static mechanism would need to determine all possible behaviors, it's generally impossible.

questions.question: A type checker is an example of a static mechanism.
questions.explanation: Type checkers analyze source code at compile time (without executing it) to enforce type safety — a classic static, white-box mechanism.

questions.question: A firewall is an example of a static mechanism.
questions.explanation: A firewall is a dynamic mechanism — it monitors and filters network traffic at runtime (black-box, runtime monitoring).

questions.question: It is generally possible to build a precise enforcer for any security property.
questions.explanation: A precise enforcer (both sound and complete) is generally impossible due to undecidability results like the Halting Problem.

questions.question: Your company's code scanner flags 200 functions as 'potentially vulnerable to SQL injection.' After manual review, only 30 actually have vulnerabilities. The scanner is:
options: Sound but not complete — it catches all issues but has false positives
Complete but not sound — it misses no safe code but has false negatives
Precise — it found the exact set of vulnerabilities
Neither sound nor complete — it's just unreliable
questions.explanation: The scanner is sound (it found all 30 real vulnerabilities — no false negatives) but not complete (170 false positives — safe functions flagged as vulnerable).

questions.question: An antivirus program is known to never flag legitimate software as malware, but occasionally misses actual malware. This antivirus is:
questions.explanation: It's complete (no false positives — never flags clean software) but not sound (has false negatives — misses some real malware).

questions.question: What is a False Positive (Type I error) in the context of a security mechanism?
options: Incorrectly flagging a secure program as insecure
questions.explanation: A False Positive (Type I error) means the mechanism incorrectly rejects or flags a secure program. It's a 'false alarm.'

questions.question: What is a False Negative (Type II error) in the context of a security mechanism?
options: Incorrectly flagging a secure program as insecure
Accepting an insecure program as if it were secure
questions.explanation: A False Negative (Type II error) means the mechanism fails to catch a real violation — it incorrectly accepts an insecure program.

questions.question: Explain why dynamic mechanisms are good for enforcing properties but bad for non-properties.
questions.answer: Dynamic mechanisms observe a single execution trace at runtime. Properties can be evaluated on individual traces, so a runtime monitor can check them. Non-properties (like noninterference) require comparing multiple traces from different executions, which a runtime monitor cannot do since it only sees one execution at a time.

questions.question: A security team must choose between a sound scanner and a complete scanner for a critical medical device. Which should they prefer and why?
questions.answer: For a critical medical device, they should prefer the sound scanner (no false negatives). Missing a real vulnerability (false negative) in a medical device could endanger lives. False positives (flagging safe code) are annoying but safe — they just mean more manual review. In safety-critical systems, it's better to over-flag than to miss real issues.

questions.question: Which of the following is a True Positive?
options: An insecure program is correctly flagged as insecure
questions.explanation: A True Positive is when the mechanism correctly identifies and flags an insecure program — the 'positive' detection is true/correct.

questions.question: Dynamic mechanisms can perfectly enforce non-property policies like noninterference.
questions.explanation: Dynamic mechanisms only see one execution trace at a time. Non-property policies like noninterference require comparing multiple traces, making them impossible to perfectly enforce dynamically.

questions.question: You need to enforce a property P on critical infrastructure. You have two tools: Tool A is sound but not complete, and Tool B is complete but not sound. What is the best strategy?
options: Use both tools together — run A first to catch all violations, then B to reduce false positives
Use neither — build a precise tool instead
questions.explanation: Using both tools together gives you the best of both worlds. Tool A (sound) ensures no real violations are missed. Tool B (complete) can help verify that flagged items are real issues, reducing false positives from Tool A.

questions.question: A compiler's type checker rejects a program that would actually be safe at runtime. This is an example of:
options: False Positive (Type I error) — the mechanism is being sound at the cost of completeness
questions.explanation: The type checker incorrectly rejects a safe program — a false positive. Type checkers are typically sound (catch all type errors) but incomplete (may reject some actually-safe programs), especially for dynamic features.

questions.question: If a mechanism has BOTH false positives and false negatives, it is:
questions.explanation: Sound means no false negatives. Complete means no false positives. Having both types of errors means the mechanism is neither sound nor complete.

questions.question: An intrusion detection system (IDS) is an example of a:
questions.explanation: An IDS is a dynamic mechanism — it monitors system/network behavior at runtime (black-box analysis) to detect potential security violations.

questions.question: Explain the Halting Problem and its relevance to static analysis mechanisms in security.
questions.answer: The Halting Problem (proved by Turing) states that no general algorithm can determine whether an arbitrary program will halt or run forever. This means no static mechanism can perfectly analyze all program behaviors — it's fundamentally impossible to build a precise static analyzer for all properties. This is why type checkers and static analyzers must be either sound (catching all issues but with false positives) or complete (no false positives but missing some issues), never both.

questions.question: A static mechanism can catch bugs at runtime.
questions.explanation: Static mechanisms analyze code without executing it (at compile/analysis time), not at runtime. Dynamic mechanisms operate at runtime.
